---
title: "Testing Bash, Python, and R"
subtitle: "Testing training — Module 03"
lang: en
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    code-block-height: 400px
---

## 🇫🇷

[🇫🇷 Version française](../../slides/03_bash_python_r.qmd)

---

## Goal

Apply the same testing logic in three different languages,
on a common example: `records.tsv`.

. . .

This module is not a framework tutorial.
To master the full syntax → **RTFM** (links in the exercises).

::: {.notes}
This module is intentionally focused on logic, not syntax. Syntax is learned from documentation — what can't be learned alone is understanding what to test and how to structure tests.

"RTFM" is not a provocation — it's an invitation. The docs for pytest, bats, and testthat are excellent and far more complete than what can be covered here.

Concrete goal: after this module, participants should be able to write their first test in each of the three languages, even imperfectly.
:::

---

## Why test scripts outside Nextflow?

The scripts `validate_records.sh`, `parse_records.py`, `stats_records.R`:

- are called by pipeline processes
- can fail silently (i.e. poor error handling)
- can be reused across multiple projects

. . .

**A bug in a script = a bug in the pipeline.**

. . .

Why *outside* Nextflow? Immediate feedback, no Nextflow overhead, targeted debugging.

::: {.notes}
Common question: "Why not just test the full pipeline with nf-test?" Answer: feedback speed. An nf-test takes at least 30 seconds to start Nextflow. A pytest or bats test takes 0.1 seconds. When debugging a script, you want the fastest possible feedback.

Second reason: if an nf-test fails, you don't know if the bug is in the Python script, the R script, the Bash script, or the Nextflow wiring. Testing each script separately lets you pinpoint the problem.

Third reason: scripts may be reused in other pipelines or contexts. If they're testable outside Nextflow, they're more robust and more reusable.
:::

---

## The scripts we will test {.smaller}

| Script | Input | What it does |
|--------|-------|--------------|
| `validate_records.sh` | TSV file | checks existence, header, numeric columns → exit 0/1 |
| `parse_records.py` | TSV file | loads, validates types and id uniqueness → DataFrame or exception |
| `stats_records.R` | DataFrame | computes mean and sd per group → data.frame |

These three scripts are called by the pipeline. Testing their contract = testing the expected behaviour.

::: {.notes}
"Contract" is the key word. Each script has a clear interface: input → output or error. The test checks that the contract is respected, not the implementation details.

`validate_records.sh`: test the exit code and the message. The internal content of the script (the awk loop, the conditions) is not what we test — we test what the caller sees.

`parse_records.py`: test that the returned DataFrame has the right shape, and that the correct exceptions are raised for invalid inputs. Don't test pandas internals — test the script's behaviour.

`stats_records.R`: test that the returned data.frame has the right columns, right values, and correct invariants. In R, errors are raised with `stop()` — check that the error message is explicit.
:::

---

## The common dataset {.smaller}

`records.tsv` — 20 valid rows · columns: id, group, value · **reference data (happy path)**

Each error file violates a single rule:

| File | Violated rule |
|------|--------------|
| `empty.tsv` | no data |
| `invalid_records.tsv` | non-numeric value |
| `duplicated_records.tsv` | non-unique id |
| `missing.tsv` | missing file |

One file = one violated rule = one diagnostic test.

::: {.notes}
The "one file per violated rule" architecture is an intentional design decision. If a single file has multiple errors, you can't tell which one caused the test failure.

`missing.tsv` is a special case: the file doesn't exist at all. It's listed here for clarity, but obviously should not be created in the repo.

For the exercises, these files are already present in `training/data/`. Participants only need to reference them in their tests.

If someone asks "why 20 rows?": it's a trade-off between having enough data for meaningful statistics (two groups A and B, several values) and staying small enough to verify manually.
:::

---

## bats — minimal syntax

```bash
@test "test name" {
    run bash script.sh arg    # runs the command, captures status + output
    [ "$status" -eq 0 ]       # exit code
    [[ "$output" =~ "text" ]] # standard output content
}
```

Documentation: <https://bats-core.readthedocs.io>

::: {.notes}
`run` is the central keyword in bats. It does three things: executes the command, captures the exit code in `$status`, and captures standard output in `$output`. Without `run`, these variables are unavailable.

`[ "$status" -eq 0 ]` uses POSIX syntax. `[[ "$output" =~ "text" ]]` uses extended bash syntax — double brackets enable regex matching with `=~`.

Common mistake: forgetting quotes around `"$status"` and `"$output"`. Without quotes, output with spaces can break the assertion.

The test name in quotes can contain spaces — that's a feature, not a bug. Write readable names like sentences: `"valid file → exit 0 and message with count"`.
:::

---

## bats — Bash

```bash
DATA="training/data"

@test "valid file → exit 0 and message with count" {
    run bash validate_records.sh "$DATA/records.tsv"
    [ "$status" -eq 0 ]                     # exit code
    [[ "$output" =~ "20 records" ]]         # content — not just the code
}

@test "non-numeric value → exit 1 and error message" {
    run bash validate_records.sh "$DATA/invalid_records.tsv"
    [ "$status" -eq 1 ]
    [[ "$output" =~ "ERROR" ]]              # check the explicit message
}
```

::: {.notes}
The first test is the happy path: valid input → exit 0 AND message with the count. Checking the message matters — a script that exits 0 but prints nothing is not useful.

The second test is an edge case: invalid input → exit 1 AND explicit error message. The assertion on `"ERROR"` verifies that the script communicates clearly what went wrong — useful for the user who needs to understand why the pipeline failed.

`DATA="training/data"` is defined outside the tests — it's a shared variable for all tests in the file. In bats, you can also use `setup()` to prepare a common environment.

Emphasise that the two assertions are complementary: exit code + content. The exit code alone is not enough.
:::

---

## bats — testing a container

```bash
@test "correct version" {
    run apptainer exec fastqc_0.12.1.sif fastqc --version
    [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
    [[ "$output" =~ "FastQC v0.12.1" ]]
}

@test "tool accessible without /home mounted" {
    run apptainer exec --no-home fastqc_0.12.1.sif fastqc --version
    [ "$status" -eq 0 ]
}
```

Same logic as testing a script — GIVEN (the image), WHEN (the command), THEN (version + exit code).

::: {style="font-size:0.55em; color:#666; margin-top:0.5em;"}
→ [Container policy](../../container-policy.qmd) — exact tag, `.def` versioned, `.sif` not committed
:::

::: {.notes}
These tests assume Apptainer is installed and the `.sif` file is available. That's not always the case in a local environment — which is why these tests are in the module 05 bonus.

The `--no-home` test matters for HPC environments where `/home` is not mounted. If a tool looks for config in `~/.config/` and `/home` isn't mounted, the test fails in production but passes in dev.

The GIVEN/WHEN/THEN logic applies identically: GIVEN = the `.sif` image available, WHEN = the `apptainer exec` command, THEN = exit 0 and the right version.

Don't spend too much time on this slide if Apptainer is not available in the training environment.
:::

---

## pytest — minimal syntax

```python
def test_name():
    assert expr                                       # simple check
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="message"):  # expected exception
        function()

@pytest.mark.parametrize("path,exc", [
    ("a.tsv", ValueError),
    ("b.tsv", FileNotFoundError),
])
def test_multi(path, exc):
    with pytest.raises(exc): function(path)           # N cases, 1 test
```

Documentation: <https://docs.pytest.org>

::: {.notes}
`pytest.raises` is the pytest equivalent of `try/except` in tests. The `match=` argument is optional but recommended: it verifies the exception message contains the expected string. Without `match`, any ValueError passes — even if the message is incomprehensible.

`@pytest.mark.parametrize` automatically generates one test per case. Generated names look like `test_multi[a.tsv-ValueError]` — readable in the error report.

Functions must start with `test_` — that's the pytest convention for automatic test discovery. Same for files (`test_*.py` or `*_test.py`).

Mention `pyproject.toml`: in this repo, `testpaths = ["training/tests/pytest"]` is configured — no need to specify the path on every run.
:::

---

## pytest — Python

```python
def test_valid_file():                              # happy path
    df = load_records(DATA / "records.tsv")
    assert df.shape == (20, 3)
    assert list(df.columns) == ["id", "group", "value"]

@pytest.mark.parametrize("path,exc", [
    ("empty.tsv",   ValueError),
    ("missing.tsv", FileNotFoundError),
])
def test_invalid_inputs(path, exc):
    with pytest.raises(exc):                            # N cases, 1 test
        load_records(DATA / path)
```

::: {.notes}
`df.shape == (20, 3)` checks both row count AND column count in one assertion.

`list(df.columns) == ["id", "group", "value"]` checks column order, not just presence. Useful if downstream code accesses columns by position.

The parametrised test separates `empty.tsv` (file exists but no data → ValueError) from `missing.tsv` (file doesn't exist → FileNotFoundError). These are two different behaviours that merit different exceptions.

`DATA` is typically defined as `pathlib.Path(__file__).parent.parent / "data"` at the top of the file. No need to hardcode absolute paths in each test.
:::

---

## testthat — minimal syntax

```r
test_that("test name", {
    expect_equal(a, b)             # equality
    expect_true(all(x >= 0))       # invariant
    expect_error(f(), "pattern")   # expected error
    expect_named(df, c("a", "b"))  # column names
})
```

Documentation: <https://testthat.r-lib.org>

::: {.notes}
`test_that` takes two arguments: a name (character string) and a code block in braces. All assertions inside the block belong to the same test.

`expect_equal` does an exact comparison for scalars and vectors. For floats, `expect_equal` uses a default tolerance (~1e-7). For a different tolerance: `expect_equal(a, b, tolerance = 1e-3)`.

`expect_error(f(), "pattern")` checks that `f()` raises an error AND that the message contains `"pattern"`. If `f()` doesn't raise an error, the test fails.

`expect_named` checks column names in a data.frame. More readable than `expect_equal(colnames(df), c("a", "b"))`.

Run R tests with `Rscript -e "testthat::test_dir('training/tests/testthat/')"` — no need for a full package.
:::

---

## testthat — R

```r
test_that("missing column → explicit error", {
    bad <- data.frame(id="S01", group="A")              # in-memory data — GIVEN
    expect_error(compute_stats(bad), "Missing columns") # WHEN + THEN
})

test_that("result has correct columns and an invariant", {
    df <- data.frame(                                   # in-memory data — GIVEN
        id = c("S01","S02"), group = c("A","B"), value = c(10, 20))
    result <- compute_stats(df)
    expect_named(result, c("group", "mean", "sd"))      # exact structure
    expect_true(all(result$mean >= 0))                  # business invariant
})
```

::: {.notes}
The advantage of in-memory data (`data.frame(...)`): no file dependency. The test is entirely self-contained. This is the standard R idiom — for R functions, create data directly in the test.

The first test checks the error case with an explicit message. `"Missing columns"` is not an exact match of the full message — it's a pattern. If the message contains that string, the test passes.

The second test combines two assertions: structure (`expect_named`) and business invariant (`expect_true(all(...))`). The invariant `mean >= 0` is a domain rule — if the computation produces a negative mean on positive values, something went wrong.

For functions that read files, use `testthat::test_path()` to build paths relative to the test directory.
:::

---

## Running the tests

```bash
# bats — from the repo root
bats training/tests/bats/

# pytest — from the repo root (testpaths configured in pyproject.toml)
pytest -v --tb=short

# testthat — from the repo root
Rscript -e "testthat::test_dir('training/tests/testthat/')"
```

::: {.callout-tip}
All three can also be run via `pre-commit run --all-files` before committing.
:::

::: {.notes}
Run each command live if possible, or at least show the expected output. Participants often get stuck not knowing which directory to be in or what flag to use.

pytest: the `-v` flag gives verbose output (one line per test). `--tb=short` shows a concise traceback on failure. Without `-v`, only failures are shown.

bats: can be run on a specific file (`bats training/tests/bats/test_validate.bats`) or on a directory (all `.bats` files in the directory).

testthat: `test_dir()` runs all `test_*.R` files in the directory. For a single file: `testthat::test_file('training/tests/testthat/test_stats.R')`.

These commands are also in the CLAUDE.md / README — participants don't need to memorise them.
:::

---

## Key takeaway

::: {.callout-important}
**Every script called by a workflow must be testable outside the workflow.**
If it cannot be tested on its own, the script does too many things.

The syntax changes. The logic does not:
test business invariants (`>= 0`, expected columns) as much as behaviour.
:::

::: {.notes}
The "testable on its own" criterion is a quality indicator. A script that can only be tested by running the full pipeline has implicit dependencies — Nextflow, other scripts, specific directories. Those dependencies make it fragile.

If someone says "my script can't be tested outside Nextflow because it uses Nextflow variables", that's the moment to discuss extracting the pure logic into a testable function, and letting the Nextflow process handle the orchestration.

The last sentence is the core message of module 03: bats, pytest, and testthat syntax is different, but the invariant we're trying to protect is the same.

Natural transition to module 04: we've tested individual scripts — now we test the Nextflow processes and workflows that call them.
:::

---

## Exercises → Modules 01, 02, 03

- `exercises/01_bash/` — bats on `validate_records.sh`
- `exercises/02_python/` — pytest on `parse_records.py`
- `exercises/03_r/` — testthat on `stats_records.R`
